topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery air hung thick and still, smelling faintly of turpentine and old varnish. Elias traced a finger over the canvas, the texture of the impasto familiar beneath his touch. It was a piece he’d poured months into—a study in muted blues and deep umbers. Then, his gaze snagged on the corner, a tiny, almost hidden detail. A precise, black line, inked just beneath the painted horizon. It wasn't part of his composition. A flicker of something cold tightened in his chest. He leaned closer, squinting. It was a stylized raven, rendered with a stark, almost surgical precision. A tattoo. He felt the familiar weight settle in his shoulders, a dull ache that had become his constant companion these last few years. He didn't move to touch it, just let his eyes absorb the intrusion, a slow, heavy acknowledgment that nothing had changed.

[story 2]

"It’s... deliberate," Clara murmured, her voice thin as tracing paper. She stood back, hands clasped loosely in front of her, watching the stark contrast between the organic chaos of the painting and the unnervingly perfect geometry of the mark. I watched her face, a practiced neutrality settling over my features. I’d seen this before. The pattern of things. I knew what this discovery meant—a piece of someone else’s history pressed onto my creation. My breath hitched, a shallow, almost imperceptible catch in my throat. I didn't voice the observation. Instead, I methodically picked up a clean brush, dipped it in a neutral grey, and began to work on a section entirely unrelated to the raven, covering the spot with a deliberate, slow sweep. The task felt endless, the required action automatic, devoid of any real excitement or distress.

[story 3]

The studio was quiet save for the hum of the ventilation. I was cleaning my palette knife, the metal cool against my palm. When I looked up at the portrait—a swirling depiction of a stormy sea—I saw it. A small, intricate knotwork, inked right where the wave crest met the foam. It was undeniably someone else’s signature, a permanent mark embedded within my effort. A profound weariness washed over me, heavy as wet wool. I set the knife down with a soft clink. There was no sudden burst of anger, no frantic urge to confront. Just a slow, steady exhale. I picked up a rag, dampened it, and began to wipe down the entire surface of the canvas, smoothing out the paint as if erasing the evidence was the only logical next step in a very long, predictable sequence.
